title: How Should Pre-Trained Language Models Be Fine-Tuned Towards Adversarial Robustness?

structured.summary: This paper proposes Robust Informative Fine-Tuning (RIFT), an adversarial fine-tuning method for pre-trained language models that leverages conditional mutual information maximization to preserve robust and generic features learned during pre-training, mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Experiments on IMDB and SNLI tasks demonstrate improved adversarial robustness and clean accuracy compared to several adversarial fine-tuning baselines.

strengths.point: Novel information-theoretic framework to mitigate catastrophic forgetting during adversarial fine-tuning
strengths.evidence: RIFT explicitly maximizes conditional mutual information I(S; T|Y) to retain robust pre-trained features, a novel approach supported by theoretical motivation and contrastive learning techniques (Section 3.2, Algorithm 1).

strengths.point: Consistent empirical improvements on adversarial robustness across tasks and models
strengths.evidence: RIFT outperforms multiple adversarial fine-tuning baselines (Adv-Base, Adv-PTWD, Adv-Mixout) on two NLP tasks (IMDB sentiment analysis, SNLI natural language inference) and two pre-trained models (BERT-base, RoBERTa-base) under strong word substitution attacks (Tables 1 and 2).

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation scope and comparison to other robustness methods
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus only on two NLP tasks with word substitution attacks and compare mainly to adversarial fine-tuning baselines, omitting other state-of-the-art robustness techniques such as randomized smoothing or certified defenses (Results section).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient theoretical justification and methodological clarity
weaknesses.evidence: The core claim about conditional mutual information balancing feature retention and separation lacks formal proof; notation and optimization steps (Equations 8-11) are dense and may confuse readers unfamiliar with mutual information estimation (Section 3.2, 3.3).

questions.question: How does RIFT perform on a broader range of NLP tasks and different pre-training objectives?
questions.motivation: Evaluating generalizability to tasks like question answering or summarization and models pre-trained with objectives other than masked language modeling would clarify the method's applicability.

questions.question: What is the computational overhead and training stability impact of RIFT compared to standard adversarial fine-tuning?
questions.motivation: Understanding runtime costs and convergence behavior is critical for practical adoption in resource-constrained settings.

critique_points.point_001: Limited evaluation scope and comparison to other robustness methods. Experiments focus only on two NLP tasks with word substitution attacks and compare mainly to adversarial fine-tuning baselines, omitting other state-of-the-art robustness techniques such as randomized smoothing or certified defenses (Results section)
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient theoretical justification and methodological clarity. The core claim about conditional mutual information balancing feature retention and separation lacks formal proof; notation and optimization steps (Equations 8-11) are dense and may confuse readers unfamiliar with mutual information estimation (Section 3.2, 3.3)
